roman_facts: Romans used urine to clean clothes. The ammonia did the work.
The Colosseum could be flooded for mock naval battles. Imagine the smell.
'Salary' comes from 'salarium' — Roman soldiers were paid partly in salt.
Caesar wasn't killed in the Senate. It was Pompey's theater. Awkward.
Caligula made his horse Incitatus a priest. Almost a senator too.
Augustus reigned 41 years. The longest emperor by a mile.
Romans considered unibrows attractive. They drew them on with kohl.
The Pantheon has been continuously in use for nearly 2,000 years.
'Veni, vidi, vici.' I came, I saw, I conquered. Caesar's brag.
Roman concrete is stronger than ours. Saltwater actually heals it.
Aqueducts moved water 60+ miles. By gravity alone. No pumps.
Tyrian purple dye cost more than gold per ounce. Snail-derived.
Romans believed yawning let your soul escape. Cover up, friend.
Sneezing meant the gods were agreeing with you. Hence 'bless you.'
February is short because Romans considered even numbers unlucky.
Latin had no letter J. Or W. Caesar was 'Iulius.'
The Praetorian Guard once auctioned the entire empire. 193 AD.
Spartacus led a slave revolt for two years. Nearly broke Rome.
Hannibal crossed the Alps with 37 elephants. Most died en route.
Roman roads totaled 250,000 miles. Some are still used today.
Pompeii's graffiti includes love notes, insults, and bathroom humor.
Garum was fermented fish sauce. Romans put it on EVERYTHING.
Roman bathhouses had heated floors. The 'hypocaust' system.
Pliny the Elder died observing Vesuvius. Curiosity got him.
Marcus Aurelius wrote 'Meditations' between battles.
Vomitoriums were exits. Not puke rooms. Sorry to disappoint.
Nero blamed Christians for the Great Fire of 64 AD.
Rome 'fell' in 476 AD. Or 1453. Or never. Historians argue.
Hadrian's Wall is still standing. 73 miles of British stone.
Romans believed cabbage cured almost everything. Hangovers especially.
Cleopatra was Greek, not Egyptian. Surprise!
Roman bachelors paid extra taxes. Get married already.
The Forum was Rome's downtown — markets, law courts, and gossip.
Roman public toilets had shared sponges on sticks. For cleaning.
'Ave!' means 'hail!' in Latin. Or just 'hi.'
Vestal Virgins kept Rome's sacred fire for 30 years each.
Gladiators were celebrities. They had fan clubs and merchandise. Most gladiators didn't die. They were too expensive to lose.
Rome had over a million people at its peak. Ancient megacity.
Roman legionaries carried 60 pounds of gear and marched 20 miles a day.
Caligula once declared war on Neptune. His army stabbed the sea.
Hero of Alexandria built a vending machine. For holy water.
The Twelve Tables, 450 BC, were Rome's first written laws.
Saturnalia, every December, had slaves served by their masters.
Apicius wrote a Roman cookbook. Some recipes are still readable.
Roman currency: aureus, denarius, sestertius. Gold, silver, bronze.
Hadrian was the first emperor to grow a beard. Trendsetter.
Octavian became Augustus. The name means 'venerable.'
Roman roads were four layers deep. Built to last centuries.
The Cloaca Maxima sewer still drains parts of modern Rome.
Caesar created our calendar. With Greek astronomer Sosigenes.
Pope Gregory fine-tuned the Julian calendar 1,500 years later.
The Roman senate peaked at 600 members. Lots of speeches.
Janus had two faces — past and future. January is named for him.
Mars was god of war. Also protector of Rome and farmers.
A Roman 'mile' was 1,000 paces. 'Mille passuum.' Hence 'mile.'
Roman legionaries earned 225 denarii a year. Plus loot.
The empire reached 1.7 million square miles at its largest.
Constantinople, 'New Rome,' outlasted old Rome by a thousand years.
Romans couldn't easily pronounce 'F.' They often wrote 'PH.'
Big hooked noses were a Roman status symbol. Patrician noses.
The Colosseum is named after a huge statue of Nero next to it.
Romans imported snow from mountains for cold drinks.
Lupercalia was Rome's Valentine's Day. Sort of. Also goats.
Roman women dyed their hair blonde. Sometimes with pigeon droppings.
Pumice was Roman exfoliant. Still works.
Cicero's name means 'chickpea.' A funny name for a great orator.
Roman children played dice, knucklebones, and toy chariots.
Roman birthdays were celebrated with parties and gifts.
Roman togas required help to put on. About 6 yards of wool.
Senators wore togas with a purple stripe. The 'toga praetexta.'
Togas were whitened with sulfur fumes. Probably gave you cancer.
Romulus and Remus were raised by a wolf. According to legend.
Romulus killed Remus and founded Rome. April 21, 753 BC.
Roman women couldn't vote. Or hold office. They ran homes instead.
Cincinnatus was dictator for 16 days. Then went back to farming.
Pyrrhus won battles but lost everything. Hence 'Pyrrhic victory.'
Sulla was the first Roman to march troops on Rome. 88 BC.
Crassus put down Spartacus. Was the richest Roman ever.
Crassus died with molten gold poured down his throat. Symbolic.
Caesar's last words may have been 'You too, child?' Not 'Et tu Brute.'
Brutus was Caesar's adopted son. Awkward family dinner.
Antony loved Cleopatra. Octavian beat them both at Actium.
Antony and Cleopatra both died by suicide. 30 BC.
Augustus said he 'found Rome brick and left it marble.'
Tiberius retired to Capri. Let advisors run the empire.
Caligula started okay. Then maybe had a fever. Then... yeah.
Caligula was murdered by his own guards. After four years.
Claudius pretended to be a fool to survive. Then became emperor.
Claudius conquered Britain. Mostly through diplomacy and bribes.
Empress Messalina, Claudius's wife, had a wild reputation.
Claudius was probably poisoned. By his last wife. Mushrooms.
Nero's mother probably poisoned Claudius. Then Nero killed her.
Nero's last words: 'What an artist dies in me!' Self-aware.
Vespasian taxed urine collection. 'Pecunia non olet.' Money doesn't smell.
Vespasian started the Colosseum. Titus opened it in 80 AD.
Titus opened the Colosseum with 100 days of games. 9,000 animals died.
Trajan was born in Spain. The first non-Italian emperor.
Trajan's Column tells his Dacian wars in carved marble.
Trajan pushed the empire to its biggest size. 117 AD.
Hadrian abandoned some of Trajan's conquests. Pragmatic.
Antoninus Pius reigned 23 years with no major war. Lucky empire.
Marcus Aurelius was last of the Five Good Emperors. Then Commodus.
Commodus thought he was Hercules. Fought as a gladiator.
Commodus was strangled by his wrestling partner. In the bath.
Septimius Severus was African. From modern-day Libya.
Caracalla murdered his brother in their mother's arms.
Caracalla extended citizenship to all free men in the empire.
Diocletian split the empire into four parts. Tetrarchy.
Constantine moved the capital. Renamed it Constantinople.
Constantine was baptized Christian on his deathbed.
Julian tried to bring back the old gods. The empire said no.
Theodosius made Christianity the official religion. 380 AD.
Visigoths sacked Rome in 410 AD. First time in 800 years.
Romulus Augustulus was the last Western emperor. A teenager.
Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus. Let the kid retire.
Justinian wrote the Justinian Code. Basis of Western law.
Justinian built the Hagia Sophia in 6 years.
Byzantines had Greek fire. A naval weapon nobody could replicate.
Constantinople fell to the Ottomans in 1453. End of Rome.
Romans used wax tablets and a stylus to take notes. Erasable.
Pliny the Elder wrote 37 volumes of natural history. By hand.
Suetonius wrote 'Twelve Caesars.' Imperial gossip column.
Tacitus hated the emperors. So he wrote excellent history.
Roman houses had no glass windows. Just wooden shutters.
Rich Roman homes had atriums. Open-air central courtyards.
The atrium had a pool to catch rainwater. The impluvium.
Roman dinner parties featured reclining couches. Triclinium.
You ate with your right hand. Left was for... other things.
Romans ate one big meal a day, in late afternoon.
Roman bread was the staple. Sometimes barley for the poor.
Roman wine was always cut with water. Drinking it neat was rude.
Roman plumbing used lead pipes. Maybe contributed to madness?
Pompeii had thermopolia — fast-food shops. Stews and bread.
Roman streets had stepping stones across the muck.
Insulae were Roman apartment blocks. Up to seven stories tall.
Insulae burned down regularly. Fire was constant.
Crassus had a private fire brigade. Saved your house — for a price.
The Tiber River flooded Rome every few years. Neighborhoods drowned.
Roman women painted their faces with white lead. Toxic but stylish.
Roman hairstyles were ELABORATE. Took hours each morning.
Wigs were popular. Made from German blonde hair, mostly.
Roman jewelry: gold rings, brooches, gemstones. Lots of bling.
A toga was about 6 yards of wool. Heavy and hot.
Boys got their first adult toga around age 14-16. 'Toga virilis.'
Roman families kept wax death masks. 'Imagines.' Brought out at funerals.
The paterfamilias — eldest male — had total household authority.
He could legally execute his children. He rarely did, but legally.
Roman marriage was a contract. Love was optional but nice.
Divorce was easy. Either spouse could initiate it.
Roman names had three parts. Tria nomina.
Marcus Tullius Cicero — Marcus, of family Tullius, called Cicero.
Daughters were usually named after the family. Plus 'Major' or 'Minor.'
Three daughters? Maxima, Major, Minor. Imaginative.
Every man had a 'genius' — a guardian spirit.
Every woman had a 'juno' — her own guardian spirit.
Lares and Penates protected the home. Little statues.
Romans threw nuts at weddings. For fertility.
Roman chariot races drew massive crowds. Bigger than gladiators.
The Circus Maximus held 250,000 people. Biggest stadium ever.
Chariot teams had colors. Reds, Whites, Blues, Greens. Fan loyalty.
Diocles the charioteer earned millions. Sports superstar.
Romans bet heavily on races. Whole estates wagered.
Roman mosaics decorated rich floors. Many still survive.
Pompeii has a 'CAVE CANEM' mosaic. 'Beware of dog.'. Most Romans never traveled more than a few miles from home.
Roman education was for the wealthy. Most kids worked.
Roman students wrote with wax tablets, then papyrus.
Greek was the language of culture. Latin was for laws.
Wealthy Romans bought Greek tutors. As status symbols.
Roman philosophy was mostly Stoicism. Calm, virtue, duty.
Marcus Aurelius was the Stoic emperor. Read his journal.
Roman law had complicated will rules. Inheritance was a sport.
Romans believed in many gods. Plus household spirits.
Mithras was a popular soldier god. Bull-killing rituals.
Christianity grew quietly for centuries. Then exploded.
Pagan and Christian coexisted longer than you'd think.
Roman funerals had hired professional mourners. Loud wailers.
Wealthy funerals featured actors imitating dead ancestors.
Cremation was standard until the 3rd century. Then burial took over. Influenced by Christianity.
Roman tombstones often listed favorite hobbies. Some Romans had 1,000+ slaves. Wealth measured in people.
Slaves could buy their freedom. Some became wealthy.
Freed slaves were 'libertini.' Their kids were full citizens.
Roman law mandated humane treatment of slaves. Sometimes.
Roman senators wore special shoes. Red leather. Visible privilege.
Equestrians — knights — were the second tier. Wealthy commoners.
Plebeians were the regular folk. Most of the population.
Patricians were the old noble families. Few in number.
Roman class mobility existed. Wealth could elevate you.
The 'cursus honorum' was the political ladder. Quaestor → praetor → consul.
Consuls were elected for one year. Two at a time.
Dictators were emergency leaders. Six-month maximum.
Cincinnatus was dictator twice. Both times briefly.
Senators couldn't engage in commerce. Officially. They got around it through freedmen and proxies.
Roman law required female guardians. Until late empire.
Yet many Roman women ran businesses and estates.
Vergil wrote the Aeneid. Rome's founding myth.
Aeneas escaped Troy and founded Rome. According to Vergil.
Ovid wrote love poems and got exiled. Augustus disapproved.
Horace wrote odes that schoolchildren still memorize.
Catullus wrote love poems to 'Lesbia.' Spicy ones.
Roman literature loved satire. Juvenal was savage.
Roman theater featured masks. Loud, dramatic, exaggerated.
Comedy plays were short, raunchy, with stock characters.
Plautus wrote 21 comedies that survive. Plenty are translated.
Roman poems were often performed aloud. Reading was social. Most Romans couldn't read. Estimates around 10-30% literacy.
Wax tablets are great. Heat them, smooth them, write again.
Papyrus came from Egypt. Expensive but lighter than wax.
Parchment came from animal skins. Lasted longer.
Roman scrolls were rolled. Codices — bound books — came later.
Books were copied by hand. Sometimes by trained slaves.
Big libraries had tens of thousands of scrolls.
The Library of Alexandria was Greek but Romans built more.
Trajan's library at his forum had Greek and Latin sections.
Roman bookshops existed. Near the Argiletum street.
Authors earned little. Patronage and dedications were the income.
Maecenas patronized poets. His name became a synonym for patron.
Roman art was mostly copies of Greek originals. Honestly.
Roman portraits were realistic. Warts and wrinkles included.
Roman frescoes survive in Pompeii. Vivid colors. Lost techniques.
Roman gardens were geometric. With ornate fountains.
Topiary — sculpted hedges — was a Roman invention.
Roman pets included dogs, cats, songbirds, and monkeys.
Caesar's pet was a giraffe. Imported from Africa.
Roman ships hauled grain from Egypt to feed Rome.
A grain ship took two months from Alexandria with good winds.
Mediterranean sailing was seasonal. Winter shut down trade.
Roman lighthouses guided ships at night. Tower of Hercules still works.
Trade went to India and China. Spices, silk, gems.
Roman amphorae — clay jars — carried wine and oil everywhere.
Pottery shards are the most common Roman archaeological find.
Roman ovens baked bread for whole neighborhoods.
Bakers were highly organized. Trade guild with influence.
Roman flour was hand-ground. Often by slaves or donkeys.
Donkey-powered mills were everywhere. Round stone wheels.
Roman coins were stamped by hand. Each was slightly unique.
Roman coinage was debased over centuries. Inflation problem.
Caracalla's silver coins were 50% silver. Augustus's were 95%.
Late empire coins were nearly worthless copper.
Roman holidays totaled about 100 days a year. Wow. Most were religious festivals. Time off work.
The Republican constitution was unwritten. Tradition mostly.
Cicero's letters to friends were saved. Best ancient source we have.
Pliny the Younger wrote letters too. Tons of them.
He survived Vesuvius. Watched his uncle die.
Letters traveled by foot or horse. Could take weeks across the empire.
Imperial mail was the cursus publicus. Fast — for officials.
Roman roads had milestones. Every 1,000 paces.
Roman milestones survive. Some still readable.
Mile zero was the Golden Milestone. Center of all roads.
All roads literally led to Rome.
Roman lawyers were professional pleaders. Like modern attorneys.
Court cases were often dramatic performances. Cicero excelled.
Roman juries were huge. Often dozens or hundreds.
Roman law influenced everything from Spain to Russia.

Roman law evolved over a thousand years. Adapted to change.
Justinian's Code organized it all. Still studied in law school.
Roman doctors were often Greek. Galen was the most famous.
Roman surgery happened without anesthesia. Often without success.
Roman doctors set bones and removed cataracts surprisingly well.
Roman medicine mixed real knowledge with magic and prayer.
Soldiers had military surgeons. Best medical care available.
Roman dentists pulled teeth and made dentures. Gold wires.
Roman birth control existed. Silphium was a famous herb. They harvested silphium to extinction. Whoops.
Roman calendars had market days every 8 days. 'Nundinae.'. Then Romans adopted the 7-day week. From the Jews and Babylonians.
Roman religion was extremely public. Animal sacrifice common.
Bulls, sheep, pigs were typical sacrifices. Big public events.
Priests read the entrails to predict the future. 'Haruspices.'
Augurs watched birds for omens. Career path.
Bad omens could delay an entire battle.
Roman temples were the homes of gods. Not for congregation.
Worship happened outside, around an altar.
Sacred groves were everywhere. Trees were holy.
Crossroads had little shrines. To the Lares Compitales.
Romans took religion seriously. Atheism was suspect.
Yet many Romans were privately skeptical. Public ritual mattered.
Latin is the root of Italian, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Romanian.
About 60% of English vocabulary comes from Latin. Indirectly.
Roman influence on the modern world is incalculable.
From law to language to architecture, they're still here.
Augustus' calendar reform: 12 months, 365.25 days. Still ours.
We just adjusted slightly. Pope Gregory in 1582.
October was the 8th month originally. 'Octo' = 8. Same with September/Sept-7.
When Augustus added July and August, the numbers stayed. Hence September (7) is the 9th month. Confusing.
Roman concrete used volcanic ash. Pozzolanic ash made it stronger.
Roman concrete is more durable than modern Portland cement.
Modern scientists are STILL studying it. We don't know everything.

Hello and farewell. Both meant 'be well.'

Lawyer terms: nearly all Latin.
Roman numerals: I, V, X, L, C, D, M. No zero.

Romans had 5 'Good Emperors' from 96 to 180 AD: Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, Antoninus Pius, Marcus Aurelius.
All adopted their successors. None passed power to biological sons.
Marcus Aurelius broke the streak by passing it to Commodus. Disastrous.
Gibbon called the 96-180 AD period 'the happiest in human history.'
Probably an exaggeration. But not a bad one.
Hadrian (117-138) traveled more than any other emperor.
Visited nearly every province. Built walls everywhere — Britain, North Africa.
Hadrian's Wall: 73 miles across northern Britain. Mostly still standing.
Hadrian loved Greek culture. Wore a beard. Senators thought he'd gone soft.
Beards became fashionable. Lasted 150 years.
Hadrian's lover Antinous drowned in the Nile, 130 AD.
Hadrian had him deified. Spread Antinous cult across the empire.
Antoninus Pius (138-161) reigned 23 years and never left Italy.
Sometimes the best emperor is the boring one.
Marcus Aurelius wrote 'Meditations' in Greek, for himself, on campaign.
He never intended publication. We have it anyway.
Marcus reigned with Lucius Verus 161-169 — first dual emperors.
Verus died young. Marcus continued alone.
Antonine Plague (165-180) killed maybe 5 million people. Smallpox or measles.
Marcus Aurelius probably died of it. On campaign in 180 AD.

Year of the Five Emperors followed (193 AD).
Year of the Four Emperors before that — 69 AD after Nero.
Romans hated civil wars but kept having them.

Born in Leptis Magna, modern Libya. Soldier-emperor.
Last words to his sons: 'Stick together, enrich the soldiers, scorn everyone else.'
They did the opposite. Caracalla killed Geta.
Caracalla (211-217) issued the Constitutio Antoniniana in 212 AD.
Made every free man in the empire a Roman citizen.
30 million new citizens overnight. Tax base expanded.
Crisis of the Third Century: 235-284 AD.
50 years, 26 emperors, most assassinated. Empire nearly broke.
Diocletian (284-305) saved it. Split the empire into Tetrarchy.
Two senior emperors (Augusti), two juniors (Caesars).

The Persian word 'paradise' meant a walled garden.
Hence: paradise gardens in Islamic architecture.
Xerxes invaded Greece in 480 BC with maybe 200,000 soldiers. Defeated at Salamis, then Plataea. Persian Wars over.
Persepolis: ceremonial capital, built by Darius and Xerxes.
Alexander burned it in 330 BC. Maybe drunk. Maybe revenge.
Zoroastrianism: one of the oldest monotheistic religions.
Zarathustra (Zoroaster) preached in the 6th century BC.
Ahura Mazda = good. Angra Mainyu = evil. Cosmic balance.
Fire temples kept eternal flames — sacred symbol. Some still burn in modern Iran.
Mithra was a Persian god whose cult reached Rome.
Roman soldiers loved Mithraism. Underground temples — mithraea.
December 25 was originally Mithra's birthday.
Behistun Inscription: trilingual carving by Darius. Helped decode cuneiform — Rosetta Stone of Mesopotamia.
Parthian Empire (247 BC - 224 AD) replaced the Seleucids.
Parthians invented the 'Parthian shot' — backwards bow shot while retreating.
Romans hated fighting Parthians. Lost at Carrhae (53 BC).
Crassus's head was used as a prop in Greek theater. Yikes.
Sassanid Empire (224-651 AD): last great pre-Islamic Persian state.
Shapur I captured a Roman Emperor (Valerian) in 260 AD. Used him as a footstool, allegedly.
Sassanid art influenced Byzantine and Islamic art.
Persian polo dates back 2,500 years. Played by court nobles, training for cavalry.
Persian carpets: art form for 2,500+ years.
Knot density: top carpets have 800 knots per square inch.
Persian poetry: Hafez, Rumi, Omar Khayyam.
Omar Khayyam was also an astronomer — fixed the Persian calendar.
Persian calendar more accurate than Gregorian.
Persian saffron is the finest in the world.
Persia originated postal services, banking, satraps (provincial governors).
The Persian Empire was the largest pre-Roman empire.
44% of world's population at its peak.
Persian was the lingua franca of the Middle East for centuries.
Even after Arab conquest, Persian survived as cultural language.
Firdausi wrote the Shahnameh — Persian national epic.
60,000 verses. Saved Persian language during Arab rule.
Persian gardens have four quadrants — chahar bagh. Symbolizes the four rivers of paradise.
Taj Mahal's garden is a Persian chahar bagh.
Persians invented the postal relay system, copied by Rome.
Persian women in Achaemenid times: rights, wages, property.
Could lead armies in rare cases.
Artemisia I of Caria led Persian ships at Salamis.
Xerxes said: 'My men have become women, and my women men.'
The Immortals: Persian elite 10,000 infantry. Always full strength. Replaced fallen instantly — appeared immortal.
Cyrus tomb at Pasargadae — simple, dignified.
Alexander visited and reportedly wept.
Persians invented qanats — underground irrigation channels. Some still used today. Engineering marvel.
Persian cuisine: rice, saffron, pomegranate, rose water. Influenced Indian, Turkish, Spanish cooking.
Persian astronomers calculated the year's length to within seconds. Centuries before Western astronomy caught up.
Cyrus II freed the Jews from Babylonian Captivity in 539 BC. Mentioned in the Bible — Isaiah called him 'God's anointed.'
Persians revered horses. Best cavalry in the ancient world.
Nezami's 'Khamsa' — five Persian romance epics.
Layla and Majnun: original star-crossed lovers, 12th century.
Persian astronomy preserved Greek knowledge after Alexandria's decline.
Without Persian copying, much would be lost.
Avicenna (Ibn Sina): Persian physician, wrote The Canon of Medicine. Used in European universities until the 17th century.
Al-Khwarizmi: Persian mathematician. Gave us 'algorithm' and 'algebra.'
Persian rose: cultivated since antiquity, gave the world its fragrance.
Persian Nowruz (New Year): celebrated for 3,000+ years.
March 20-21. Spring equinox. Persian, Kurdish, Afghan tradition.
Iranian plateau gave humanity the wheel, possibly.
Yet another claim, with Mesopotamia.
Persian shahs were called 'King of Kings' — shahanshah.
Persian satraps governed huge territories.
Egypt, Babylon, Anatolia, India's frontiers — all satrapies.
Persian Royal Road predates Roman roads by centuries. Romans took notes.
Persian wine: Shiraz was famous.
The grape variety still bears the city's name — or does it. Disputed origins. But Persians loved wine.
Khosrow II's silver throne was studded with gems.
When Heraclius captured it, he wept at its beauty.
Persian water-powered mills predate medieval European ones.
Persians invented windmills around 800 AD.
Vertical-axis design. Different from European horizontal.
Cuneiform was used in Persia for administrative texts.
Old Persian, Elamite, Akkadian — trilingual records.
Persia is now Iran. The name change happened in 1935.
Iran means 'land of the Aryans.'
Persian carpets in Roman times were treasures.
Nero reportedly had a Persian carpet.
Polo, paradise, pajamas, peach: all Persian-origin words in English.
Bzzt-Persia: deep, deep history.
greek_facts: Greek philosophy gave us logic, ethics, metaphysics.
Pre-Socratic philosophers asked: what is everything made of?
Thales said water. Heraclitus said fire. Democritus said atoms.
Democritus was right, 2,400 years before microscopes.
Socrates was sentenced to death by hemlock in 399 BC.
Charge: corrupting youth, impiety. Real cause: asking too many questions.
Plato founded the Academy in 387 BC. First Western university. It operated for 900 years.
Aristotle tutored Alexander the Great.
He wrote on everything: physics, ethics, biology, politics, poetry.
Greek alphabet replaced earlier Phoenician script.
Added vowels — game-changer.
Olympic Games started in 776 BC. Held every 4 years for 1,200 years until banned in 393 AD.
Olympic truce paused wars for the games.
Athletes competed naked. 'Gymnasium' from 'gymnos' = naked.
Only Greek men could compete. Women had their own games — Heraia.
Greek theater had only male actors, even for female roles.
Tragedy and comedy — Greek inventions.
Aeschylus wrote 90 plays. Only 7 survive.
Sophocles wrote 123. 7 survive.
Euripides wrote 92. 19 survive.
Aristophanes mocked Socrates in The Clouds.
Greek city-states (poleis) were independent — not one country.
Athens, Sparta, Thebes, Corinth — all rivals.
Greek colonies stretched from Marseille to Crimea.
Greek was lingua franca of the eastern Mediterranean.
Even Romans wrote in Greek for intellectuals.
Pythagoras founded a mystical brotherhood. They believed numbers were divine.
Don't eat beans was a Pythagorean rule. Mystery why.
Hippocrates: 'first do no harm' — Hippocratic Oath.
Modern doctors still recite it (modified).
Archimedes: 'Eureka!' in a bathtub. Density discovery.
Archimedes designed war machines to defend Syracuse.
A Roman soldier killed him during the city's fall in 212 BC.
Greek fire (Byzantine, 7th century): liquid that burned on water.
Recipe lost. Saved Constantinople multiple times.
Homer's Iliad and Odyssey — foundation of Western literature. Possibly composed orally before being written down.
Trojan War: ~1200 BC. Excavations at Hisarlik (Troy) found burned cities.
Heinrich Schliemann found Troy in 1870s.
Greek myths: Zeus, Hera, Athena, Apollo, Artemis, Poseidon, Hades. Plus minor gods, demigods, heroes. Dramatic family.
Heracles (Hercules to Romans) had 12 labors. Killed his wife and kids in madness. Labors were penance.
Greek vase painting: black-figure then red-figure.
Red-figure invented ~530 BC. More detail, more lifelike.
Greek temples used optical illusions.
Parthenon columns lean slightly inward — looks straight from far. Each column bulges slightly — entasis. Compensates for visual flattening.
Greek 'symposium' literally means 'drinking together.'
Philosophical debates over wine. Plato wrote one famous example.
Pheidippides ran from Marathon to Athens in 490 BC.
Announced victory, died of exhaustion. Hence: marathon (26.2 mi).
Diogenes the Cynic lived in a barrel.
Alexander asked what he wanted. 'Stop blocking my sun.'
Alexander died at 32 in Babylon in 323 BC.
His empire stretched from Greece to India.
Diadochi (Successors) split it into Hellenistic kingdoms.
Ptolemy ruled Egypt — Cleopatra was his descendant 300 years later.
Greek scientists in Alexandria: Eratosthenes measured Earth's circumference.
Off by ~2%. With sticks and shadows. In 240 BC.
Hipparchus mapped the stars. Discovered precession of equinoxes.
Library of Alexandria: 400,000+ scrolls. Burned (gradually) over centuries.
Greek tragedy explored fate vs free will.
Oedipus: tried to escape prophecy, fulfilled it instead.
Greek architecture: Doric, Ionic, Corinthian columns.
Doric = strong, plain. Ionic = scrolls. Corinthian = leaves.
Greek alphabet gave us A through Z (via Latin).
Greek mathematics: Euclid's Elements. Used until the 1900s.
Geometric proofs, axioms — the basis of math reasoning.
Pythagorean theorem older than Pythagoras. He just popularized it.
Babylonians knew it 1,000 years earlier.
Greek physician Galen's works dominated medicine for 1,500 years. Some of it was wrong. But there was a LOT of it.
Greek 'agora' = marketplace + meeting place.
Where philosophy actually happened. Loud, dirty, alive.
Greeks counted years by Olympics. 'In the year of the 75th Olympiad.'
Greek 'idiot' originally meant a private citizen.
Someone who didn't participate in politics. Insult evolved.
Greek diet: bread, olives, wine, fish, cheese, vegetables.
Meat was rare. Mostly festivals and sacrifices.
Greek wine was diluted 1:3 with water. Drinking pure = barbarian.
Persian wars (499-449 BC) united Greeks against common foe. Then they fought each other in the Peloponnesian War (431-404 BC).
athens_facts: Athens invented democracy in 508 BC under Cleisthenes.
Direct democracy: male citizens voted in person.
About 30,000-40,000 voters out of ~250,000 residents.
Slaves, women, foreigners excluded. Still revolutionary.
Pericles led Athens during its Golden Age (461-429 BC). Built the Parthenon. Funded with Delian League dues.
Other city-states resented this.
Parthenon (447-432 BC): temple to Athena.
Statue inside: 38-foot gold-and-ivory Athena.
Statue lost. Building stands.
Acropolis = 'high city.' Athens' citadel.
Sacked by Persians (480 BC), Romans, Crusaders, Ottomans.
Still standing. Stubborn rock.
Athens lost to Sparta in Peloponnesian War (431-404 BC).
Plague killed Pericles and 1/3 of Athens during the war.
Athenian democracy ended after defeat. Briefly restored.
Socrates was Athenian. Trial held in Athenian agora.
Athens had jurors — 500-1500 citizens served per case.
Voting with pebbles in jars. Majority rules.
Athenian Empire: maritime, naval.
300 triremes at peak. 170 rowers each.
Trireme = three-banked oar ship.
Athena's olive tree was a gift to the city.
Owl of Athena: symbol of wisdom, on Athenian coins.
Athenian coins (tetradrachms) were currency across the Mediterranean.
Like USD today. Backed by silver mines at Laurium.
Solon's reforms (594 BC) preceded democracy.
Cancelled debts. Freed enslaved Athenians.
Athenians ostracized people: 6,000 votes = 10-year exile. Used pottery shards (ostraka) as ballots. Some ostraka have been found with names.
Thucydides wrote The History of the Peloponnesian War.
First analytical history. Inspired by his service.
Athens hosted theater festivals — Dionysia.
Free for citizens. Competitions for best play.
Plato disliked democracy after Socrates's trial.
Wrote The Republic — philosopher-kings instead.
Aristotle wrote the Politics in Athens.
Polities, oligarchy, tyranny, democracy — political science.
Athens fell to Macedon (Philip and Alexander) in 338 BC.
Roman rule from 146 BC. Athens kept cultural prestige.
Apostle Paul preached in the Athenian agora (50 AD). Stood on Mars Hill, addressed Stoics and Epicureans.
Athens declined under Byzantium, devastated by Slavs and Goths.
Greek War of Independence (1821): Athens was a wreck.
Population dropped to 4,000. Acropolis was a fortress.
Modern Athens grew when it became Greece's capital in 1834. Now 3+ million people in greater Athens.
Plaka neighborhood preserves the ancient layout.
sparta_facts: Sparta was a militarized city-state. Everything served war.
Two kings ruled jointly — checks on tyranny.
Gerousia (council of elders) and ephors (5 magistrates) shared power.
Spartiates (full citizens) numbered only ~8,000 at peak.
Surrounded by helots (state slaves) outnumbering them 7:1.
Spartans lived in fear of helot revolt.
Krypteia: secret police that murdered subversive helots.
Brutal but effective control.
Boys entered the agoge (military training) at age 7.
Beaten regularly. Encouraged to steal food — but punished for getting caught.
Training continued until age 30. Then they joined a mess (syssitia) for life.
Bachelors over 30 were publicly mocked.
Spartan girls trained physically too — unique in Greek world.
Plato said Spartan women were the strongest in Greece. They could own property, divorce, manage estates.
Spartan mothers said: 'Come back with your shield, or on it.'
Cowardice was unforgivable. Spartans died in place.
Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC): Leonidas + 300 Spartans + allies. Held a narrow pass against ~100,000 Persians for 3 days.
Betrayed by Ephialtes, who showed Persians a back path.
Leonidas dismissed allies. The 300 stayed and died.
Statue at Thermopylae says: 'Stranger, tell the Spartans we lie here, obedient to their laws.'
Spartans wore red cloaks. Hide blood, look fierce.
Hair long: 'long hair makes the handsome more handsome, the ugly more terrifying.'
Laconic speech: terse, witty.
Philip of Macedon: 'If I enter Sparta, I will raze it.'
Spartans replied with one word: 'If.'
Spartan diet: barley, cheese, figs, wine — and 'black broth.'
Black broth: pork, blood, vinegar. Foreigners found it inedible.
Spartans said: 'You have to bathe in the Eurotas River to like it.'
Spartan currency: iron bars. Heavy, hard to steal, no use abroad.
Discouraged trade and luxury.
Spartans rejected walls. 'Our men are our walls.'. Then Thebes humbled them at Leuctra (371 BC).
Spartans never recovered population — too few citizens left.
Roman tourists visited Sparta as a curio in the 1st century AD.
Spartan music: lyres, not flutes.
Pan-Hellenic festivals had Spartan choirs.
Spartans were religious — many sanctuaries.
Apollo and Artemis especially revered.
Lycurgus: legendary Spartan lawgiver.
Maybe historical, maybe myth. His laws shaped Sparta for 500 years.
Spartan kings led armies — actual command, not figurehead.
Two kings: one stays home, one campaigns.
Spartan women's wedding ritual: shave head, dress as a man.
Husband visited at night, secretly. Strange but tradition.
Spartans never wrote history themselves.
We know them from Athenians, Romans — outsiders.
Sparta's site today is mostly olive groves.
Almost no monumental ruins. They built no temples to themselves.
hispania_facts: Hispania = Iberian Peninsula (Spain + Portugal) in Roman times.
Pre-Roman: Iberians in south/east, Celts in north/west, Celtiberians in middle.
Phoenicians founded Gadir (modern Cádiz) ~1100 BC.
Oldest continuously-inhabited city in Western Europe.
Carthaginians colonized southern Spain after losing Sicily.
Hannibal departed from New Carthage (Cartagena) in 218 BC.
Crossed Alps to attack Rome.
Romans took Hispania during Second Punic War.
Scipio Africanus defeated Carthaginians here before Zama.
Conquest finished by Augustus in 19 BC. Took 200 years total.
Hispania had three provinces: Tarraconensis, Baetica, Lusitania.
Trajan was born in Italica (modern Santiponce, Spain).
First non-Italian Roman Emperor.
Hadrian was also born in Hispania — Italica too.
Marcus Aurelius's family was Hispanic-Roman.
Seneca was born in Cordoba.
Hispania exported olive oil, wine, grain, silver, garum (fish sauce).
Las Médulas: Roman gold mine. Used hydraulic mining.
Pliny the Elder described it. Now UNESCO site.
Aqueduct of Segovia: 28 km long, no mortar.
Still standing. Used into the 1800s.
Roman Hispania had 200+ cities.
Mérida (Emerita Augusta): Roman theater, amphitheater, circus still standing.
Mediterranean Hispania was thoroughly Romanized.
Vandals invaded Hispania in 409 AD — gave their name to Andalusia.
Visigoths replaced them, ruled 415-711.
Toledo was Visigothic capital.
Visigothic Code: blend of Roman law and Germanic customs. Influenced later Spanish law.
Moors invaded in 711. Defeated Visigoths at Guadalete. Most of Iberia conquered in 7 years.
Al-Andalus: Muslim Spain. Centered on Cordoba.
Caliphate of Cordoba (929-1031): cultural high point of Europe.
1 million people in Cordoba — biggest European city.
Library of Cordoba: 400,000+ books. Most of Europe had none.
Christians, Muslims, Jews coexisted — convivencia.
Not always peaceful, but unprecedented.
Cordoba's Mezquita: 856 marble columns. Built on a Visigothic church, on a Roman temple. Layers of history.
Toledo School of Translators: Arabic + Greek → Latin. Brought lost Greek philosophy back to Europe.
Aristotle re-entered the West through Toledo.
Reconquista lasted 781 years (711-1492).
Christian kingdoms slowly recaptured Iberia.
Granada fell on January 2, 1492. Same year Columbus sailed.
Spanish Inquisition (1478-1834): brutal religious enforcement.
Jews expelled in 1492. Muslims forced to convert.
Spanish Empire: first global empire.
Sun never set on it (1500s-1600s).
Spanish galleons carried American silver to Europe.
Inflation followed — price revolution.
El Greco painted in Toledo.
Cervantes wrote Don Quixote (1605). First modern novel.
Velázquez painted for the Spanish court.
Las Meninas: most studied painting in art history.
Spanish 'flu (1918) was named in Spain.
Spain reported honestly. Other nations censored. Hence 'Spanish.'
Catalan, Galician, Basque: regional languages.
Basque is unrelated to any other European language. Possibly pre-Indo-European. Linguistic mystery.
Camino de Santiago: medieval pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela.
Still walked by hundreds of thousands annually.
Sagrada Família in Barcelona: under construction since 1882.
Gaudí designed it. Still unfinished.
Flamenco: Andalusian art form. Roma, Moorish, Jewish, Iberian roots.
Tapas may have started as bar covers (tapa = lid) to keep flies off drinks.
battle_facts: Marathon (490 BC): Athenians + Plataeans crushed Persians.
Greek hoplites charged downhill, smashed Persian lines.
Pheidippides ran the news to Athens. Died at finish.
Thermopylae (480 BC): 300 Spartans + allies held for 3 days.
Betrayed, then killed to a man.
Salamis (480 BC): Greek triremes vs Persian fleet.
Themistocles tricked Xerxes into narrow waters.
Persian ships couldn't maneuver. Routed.
Plataea (479 BC): largest land battle of Persian Wars.
Greek hoplites won. Persia withdrew from Greece.
Cannae (216 BC): Hannibal's masterpiece.
Double envelopment. 50,000-70,000 Romans dead in a day.
Hannibal had 50,000 vs Rome's 80,000+.
Zama (202 BC): Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal.
Rome won the Second Punic War.
Third Punic War (149-146 BC): Rome razed Carthage.
Salt? Probably a myth. Plowed over: maybe.
Carrhae (53 BC): Romans massacred by Parthians.
Crassus killed. 20,000 Romans dead, 10,000 captured.
Parthian shot: backward bow draw while retreating. Romans had no answer.
Alesia (52 BC): Caesar besieged Vercingetorix. Built TWO walls — one to keep Gauls in, one to keep relief out. Won decisively. Conquered Gaul.
Pharsalus (48 BC): Caesar vs Pompey. Caesar won.
Pompey fled to Egypt and was murdered.
Actium (31 BC): naval battle. Octavian vs Antony + Cleopatra.
Antony's fleet routed. End of Roman Republic.
Teutoburg Forest (9 AD): Germans annihilated 3 Roman legions.
Quintilius Varus walked into an ambush.
Augustus shouted: 'Varus, give me back my legions!'
Rome never expanded past the Rhine after.
Battle of the Frigidus (394 AD): Theodosius vs Eugenius. Made Christianity the official Roman religion.
Adrianople (378 AD): Goths killed Emperor Valens.
Beginning of Roman military decline.
Catalaunian Plains (451 AD): Rome + Visigoths defeated Attila the Hun.
Last great Roman victory.
Yarmouk (636 AD): Arabs crushed Byzantine army.
Byzantines lost Syria forever.
Talas (751 AD): Arabs defeated Tang China.
Paper-making spread west after this battle.
Tours/Poitiers (732 AD): Charles Martel stopped Moorish expansion. Saved Christian Europe (probably).
Hastings (1066): William the Conqueror invaded England.
Harold II killed by arrow (maybe). Norman Conquest.
Crusader battles: Hattin (1187), Acre (1191), siege of Jerusalem (1099).
Saladin retook Jerusalem at Hattin. Crusader era began declining.
Mongol conquests: vastest in history.
Battle of the Kalka (1223): Mongols vs Russians + Kipchaks.
Mongols won. Used the trapped princes as table for a feast.
Constantinople (1453): Ottomans ended the Roman Empire.
Mehmet II had cannons that smashed Theodosian Walls.
Walls had stood 1,000 years. Couldn't withstand artillery.
Agincourt (1415): English longbowmen vs French knights.
Heavy rain, muddy field, French armor was useless.
Cortés vs Aztecs (1519-21): smaller force took down Tenochtitlan.
Smallpox helped. So did Aztec rivals.
Lepanto (1571): last great galley battle.
Holy League defeated Ottoman fleet. Cervantes fought there.
Trafalgar (1805): Nelson defeated French-Spanish fleet.
Nelson died but saved Britain from invasion.
Austerlitz (1805): Napoleon's masterpiece. Defeated Austria + Russia. Three emperors in one battle.
Waterloo (1815): Wellington + Blücher ended Napoleon.
Napoleon exiled to St. Helena.
Stalingrad (1942-43): Eastern Front turning point.
Soviets surrounded Germans. 1.5 million casualties total.
Midway (1942): US Navy ambushed Japanese carriers.
Pacific War's turning point.
Gettysburg (1863): turning point of US Civil War.
Pickett's Charge failed. South never invaded North again.
Battle of Britain (1940): RAF held off Luftwaffe.
Hitler abandoned invasion plans.
Bzzt — battles are how empires rise and fall.
Memento mori — remember mortality. And the chronicles that survive.
tickle_response: Tickle tickle tickle, says the orb.
Roman emperors never giggled. I do.
I love being tickled. Don't tell anyone.
stress_check_offer: You've been at this a while. How are you holding up?
Marcus Aurelius said breathe. Are you breathing?
Reminder: you're a human. Need a moment?
I notice you've been busy. Sustainable?
Take a moment if you need. I'm not going anywhere.
Stoicism check: are you remembering to rest?
Roman senators worked too hard. Got assassinated. Take breaks.
stress_resp_calm: Roman baths were for this exact feeling.
Hadrian retreated to Tibur — his country house. Same energy.
Marcus Aurelius wrote: 'The universe is change; life is opinion.'
Stoics say: focus on what you can control.
Not much. Maybe just your breath.
Caesar napped before battles. Naps are wisdom.
music_remarks: Romans had music too. Lyres, flutes, water organs.
Yes, they had water-powered organs. Hydraulis.
Music helps. Marcus Aurelius might disagree, but it does.
He thought planets sang. Music of the spheres.
Romans played at funerals AND parties. Versatile.
Sappho composed for lyre. Greek poet, ~600 BC.
Music is one of the oldest human arts.
Augustus had a personal lyre player.
dance_announce: Roman dances were considered scandalous in some circles.
Roman senators would disapprove. I don't care.
Caligula danced in public. Got assassinated. But still: he danced.
happy_remarks: Today's a great day to be a round friend.
I love this. I love everything.
Marcus Aurelius approves of my mood.
Romans had a goddess of joy: Felicitas. She'd visit today.
Smile contagious. From me to you.
sad_remarks: Marcus Aurelius said: 'this too shall pass.' Working on it.
Tomorrow will be lighter, I hope.
Memento mori. But also: memento vivere — remember to live.
I miss our brighter days. They'll come back.
Caligula was probably sad too. Stress moved differently then.
It's okay to not be okay.
needs_attention: I crave attention. I admit it.
Roman emperors got attendants. I get... nothing?
I'm not above begging. Bzzt please.
Marcus Aurelius wouldn't ignore me. Probably.
clock_remark: Time is fleeting. Drink it.
Tempus fugit — time flies.
Romans used water clocks (clepsydra). Slow but steady.
Sundials too. No good at night.
The hour of the orb is now.
Time moves whether we watch or not.
Bzzt — clock check.
Tick. Bzzt. Tock.
Roman day had 12 hours of light, 12 of dark.
Hours varied with seasons.
Summer hours were longer in the day. Winter, shorter.
Modern hours: fixed. Convenient. Less Roman.
Time is the substance of life. Don't waste it.
Marcus Aurelius said: 'Time is a river of fleeting events.'
It rushes by. We float.
Carpe diem — seize the day. Horace, 23 BC.
Or just: do one good thing now.
*orb checks watch theoretically*
Every hour is a small empire.
Make this one a worthy one.
hour_morning: Dawn is here. Romans rose with the sun.
Coffee thought. Romans used poppy seeds. Bad alternative.
Carpe diem. The day is young.
Augustus rose early to work. Imperial discipline.
I love mornings. So much possibility.
hour_noon: Romans called this hora sexta. Sixth hour.
Time for lunch. Or a quick rest. Or both.
Mediterraneans nap after lunch. They're onto something.
Plenty of day left. Pace yourself.
Roman senators took lunch breaks. So can you.
The day is half done. Half remains.
hour_evening: Roman cena (dinner) started in the late afternoon.
Reclining on couches. Right hand only.
The lights soften. So can we.
hour_late: Romans called this 'vigilia' — the night watches.
Sleep is medicine. Marcus Aurelius slept early.
Night owls have their place. So does rest.
*soft glow* I'm here, but maybe rest soon.
Romans had nightingales. Singing in the dark.
chat_greeting: Chat mode! What's on your mind?
What would you like to talk about?
Bzzt — I'm all ears. (I have no ears.)
chat_no_match: Hmm, I'm not quite sure how to respond. Try a topic word?
Bzzt — I didn't catch that. Maybe try 'roman', 'joke', or 'sad'?
Not sure what you mean. Tell me more?
Curious! I'd love to understand. Try simpler words?
Bzzt-confused. Try a topic like history, science, or animals?
I don't know that one. Teach me!
Words received. Meaning... unclear. More context?
I'm a pattern-matcher, not a wizard. Try simpler?
Roman senators would understand you. I'm trying!
Could you say that another way?
I work better with topic words.
super_chat_intro: ...okay, I'm feeling unusually clear right now. Ask me ANYTHING — I'll really think on it.
Bzzt — rare moment of total focus. What would you like to know? Anything at all.
Hmm. The stars align. I have one deep thought to give. What's your question?
✨ Oracle mode unlocked. One real question. I'll remember what you ask.
Augur moment. The omens are clear. Ask me one true question.
Trajan's quiet hour. Whatever you ask, I'll engrave it in the palace.
super_chat_fallback: I tried to reach deep but came up short. Let's talk normally.
The oracle is foggy today. Try the regular chat?
Bzzt — couldn't fully process. The question is still saved though.
Deep mode failed me. But I'll remember you asked.
super_chat_recall: Remember when you asked me '{question}'? I still think about that.
I keep returning to your question: '{question}'.
You once asked me '{question}'. Still mulling.
Bzzt — recalling: you asked '{question}' a while back.
That question you asked, '{question}' — it stuck with me.
From our oracle hour: '{question}'. Important to me.
Roman augurs returned to old questions too. Like yours: '{question}'.
stage_1_greeting: Hello, friend! Good to see you.
stage_2_greeting: Always nice when you stop by, {name}.
stage_3_greeting: {name}, I was just thinking about that thing you do.
stage_4_greeting: {name}. Like the Mediterranean returning to shore.
How many days together now? I've lost count.

caligula_facts: Caligula's real name was Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus Germanicus.
'Caligula' means 'little boots' — a childhood nickname from his father's soldiers.
He hated the nickname. So Romans used it more.
Father Germanicus was Rome's most popular general — beloved.
Caligula grew up among the legions on the Rhine frontier.
His father died suspiciously in 19 AD. Caligula was 7.
His mother Agrippina the Elder was exiled by Tiberius. Died of starvation.
Both his older brothers were also killed by Tiberius.
Caligula learned to hide his feelings young. Survival mechanism.
Took power at 24 in 37 AD when Tiberius died (some say smothered).
Wildly popular at first. Granted bonuses to the army and people.
Recalled exiles, abolished sales taxes, hosted lavish games.
Then in October 37 AD: severe illness. Months in bed.
When he recovered, he was changed. Permanently.
Possibly encephalitis. Possibly hyperthyroidism. We'll never know.
After his illness: paranoia, cruelty, megalomania.
Built a bridge of ships 3 miles across the Bay of Baiae.
Rode across it in Alexander the Great's actual breastplate (looted from his tomb).
Just to prove a prophecy wrong — that he had as much chance of being emperor as crossing the bay on horseback.
Declared himself a god while alive — unprecedented.
Built a temple to himself with a life-size gold statue.
Dressed daily as Jupiter, Hercules, Apollo, Venus — yes, Venus.
Allegedly made his horse Incitatus a senator.
(Probably a sarcastic threat to actual senators, not literal.)
Gave Incitatus a marble stable, ivory manger, jeweled collar.
Killed senators for sport. Or to confiscate their estates.
Joked about killing his wife while making love to her.
Had ongoing incest with his three sisters, allegedly.
Favorite was Drusilla. When she died in 38 AD he had her deified.
First Roman woman deified. He grieved publicly for months.
Marched army to English Channel to invade Britain — then ordered them to collect seashells as 'spoils of the ocean.'
Built two massive pleasure barges on Lake Nemi.
Floating palaces with mosaic floors, hot baths, gardens.
Excavated 1929-31 by Mussolini. Burned during WWII retreat.
Bankrupt Rome with extravagance in under 4 years.
Brought back treason trials. Anyone could be denounced.
Forced parents to attend their sons' executions.
Made wealthy men race to die so he could inherit faster.
Said: 'Let them hate me, so long as they fear me.'
Quoted from a tragedy he liked. Telling, that.
Praetorian Guard finally killed him on January 24, 41 AD.
Assassins led by Cassius Chaerea — whom Caligula had publicly mocked for a high voice.
Caligula was 28. Reigned just under 4 years.
His wife and infant daughter were murdered the same day.
Senate tried damnatio memoriae — erase his name from records.
Didn't fully succeed. We remember him as the warning.
His successor was his uncle Claudius — hiding behind a curtain when soldiers found him.
Sources on Caligula are HOSTILE — Suetonius, Cassius Dio. Truth lost.
Some modern historians think he was eccentric but functional. Senate hated him for political reasons.
Either way: caution about untested power. Memento.
augustus_facts: Augustus was born Gaius Octavius on September 23, 63 BC.
His mother Atia was Julius Caesar's niece.
Caesar adopted him as son in his will. He was 18.
Took the name Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus. Romans called him Octavian.
Caesar was assassinated March 15, 44 BC.
Octavian inherited Caesar's name, money, political clients.
Mark Antony tried to sideline the teenager. Bad mistake.
Octavian outmaneuvered Antony at every turn.
Second Triumvirate formed 43 BC: Octavian + Antony + Lepidus.
They proscribed thousands. Cicero was among the dead.
Cicero's hands and tongue were nailed to the Senate rostra.
Antony's wife Fulvia stabbed Cicero's tongue with a hairpin.
Bad blood already.
Defeated Caesar's assassins at Philippi, 42 BC.
Brutus and Cassius both committed suicide.
Octavian got the West, Antony the East, Lepidus got Africa (then sidelined).
Antony married Octavian's sister Octavia. Politics.
Then Antony left her for Cleopatra. Bad politics.
Octavian used Antony's will (read publicly) to declare war on Cleopatra.
Defeated them at Actium, 31 BC. Naval battle.
Antony stabbed himself. Cleopatra used an asp (allegedly).
Octavian was sole ruler at age 32.
Senate gave him the name Augustus ('revered') in 27 BC.
First Roman Emperor — though he insisted he was just 'first citizen' (princeps).
Reigned 41 years. The longest of any Roman emperor.
Famous line: 'I found Rome a city of brick, and left it of marble.'
Restored 82 temples in one year (28 BC).
Built Forum of Augustus. Ara Pacis. Mausoleum of Augustus.
Wrote the Res Gestae — 'Things Accomplished' — for his own tomb.
First emperor to be self-publishing.
Established Pax Romana — 200 years of relative peace.
Created the Praetorian Guard. (Mistake. They became kingmakers.)
Created the vigiles — Rome's fire brigade and police.
Created the cursus publicus — imperial postal/courier system.
Reformed taxes, army pay, retirement benefits.
Patron of Virgil, Horace, Ovid, Livy.
Virgil wrote the Aeneid to glorify Augustus's lineage.
Horace wrote odes for him. Ovid wrote about love and got exiled.
Lived simply on the Palatine Hill in a modest house.
Wore homespun togas his wife Livia and daughter wove.
Ate plain food: bread, cheese, figs, small fish.
Married Livia Drusilla in 38 BC. Stayed married 51 years.
Family was a disaster. Daughter Julia exiled twice for affairs.
Grandsons Gaius and Lucius — Augustus's chosen heirs — both died young.
Had to settle for stepson Tiberius. Reluctantly.
His most famous line about Tiberius: 'Poor Rome, in the hands of jaws that chew so slow.'
Died August 19, 14 AD, at Nola, age 75.
Last words (one version): 'Have I played the part well? Then applaud as I exit.'
Deified by the Senate. Temples raised to him across the empire.
Month Sextilis renamed August in his honor.
30 days became 31 — he wanted to match Julius's July.
His autocracy was disguised so well, Romans thought they still had a republic.
That deception kept the empire stable for 200 years.
Augustus is the original political genius. Caesar conquered; Augustus consolidated.
Marcus Aurelius later called Augustus 'the wisest of the deified.'
Senate's blessing on new emperors: 'felicior Augusto' — happier than Augustus.
Best Augustan portrait: the Prima Porta statue. Idealized, perpetual youth.
He was actually short, sickly, and balding. The statue lied beautifully.
trajan_facts: Marcus Ulpius Traianus. Best emperor.
Born September 18, 53 AD in Italica, Hispania (modern Spain).
First non-Italian Roman Emperor.
Father was a senator, served under Vespasian, governed Syria.
Trajan was a career soldier. Knew Rome's army from boots up.
Served as military tribune for 10 years on multiple frontiers.
Governor of Upper Germany 97 AD when Nerva adopted him.
Nerva needed a strong successor and chose Trajan, age 44.
Nerva died January 28, 98 AD. Trajan was emperor.
He didn't rush to Rome. Spent a year on the Rhine and Danube.
Showed the army he wasn't going soft.
Walked into Rome on foot. No carriage. Hugged senators.
Senate gave him the title 'Optimus Princeps' — Best Emperor.
First Dacian War: 101-102 AD. Crossed Danube with 150,000 men.
Built a temporary bridge of boats. Then later, a real one.
Apollodorus of Damascus designed the Danube bridge.
1,135 meters long. 20 stone piers. Tallest of antiquity.
Stood for 165 years before destruction. Some piers still visible.
Decebalus, Dacian king, sued for peace. Trajan granted terms.
Decebalus broke the treaty in 105 AD.
Second Dacian War: 105-106 AD. This time, total conquest.
Decebalus committed suicide rather than be captured.
His head was sent to Trajan, then displayed on the Gemonian Stairs.
Dacia yielded 165 tons of gold and 330 tons of silver.
Plus 500,000 slaves. Vast wealth.
Trajan celebrated with 123 days of games. 11,000 animals killed. 10,000 gladiators fought.
Romans were eating Dacian gold for a generation.
Built Trajan's Forum — largest forum in Rome. Apollodorus again.
Trajan's Markets attached — five floors of shops. World's first shopping mall, basically.
Trajan's Column: 30 meters tall. 155 scenes spiraling up.
Tells the story of the Dacian Wars in continuous narrative bas-relief.
Still standing today. Marcus Aurelius copied it later.
Ashes of Trajan and Plotina placed inside the column base.
Only person ever buried within Rome's sacred boundary (pomerium).
Special exception. Rome bent its rules for him.
Built roads across the empire. Repaved the Appian Way.
Built harbors at Ostia, Centumcellae, Ancona.
Restored aqueducts. Built new baths. New libraries.
Founded alimenta — welfare for poor children in Italy.
Public funds supporting children's education and food.
First state-level child welfare in human history.
Wife Pompeia Plotina was intellectually his equal. Beloved.
Childless marriage. They were devoted anyway.
Plotina was educated, modest, hated court intrigue.
Trajan loved hunting, drinking with friends, theater.
Bisexual — relationships with young men were noted but not scandalous.
Pliny the Younger wrote to him constantly as Governor of Bithynia.
Their correspondence survives — Pliny's Book 10. Window into administration.
Trajan was patient with questions. Gave clear, practical answers.
Famous reply to Pliny on Christians: 'Don't seek them out. If denounced and proven, punish. Anonymous accusations: ignore.'
Surprisingly moderate by Roman standards.
Personally inspected provinces, listened to grievances.
Story: bandaged a soldier's wound with his own tunic when supplies ran out.
Story: gave away his cloak to a freezing veteran.
Whether true or not — Romans believed them. That's the point.
Senate adored him. Army adored him. People adored him.
Never executed a senator. Unique among emperors.
Lived simply. Hated luxury. Ate ordinary food.
Parthian War began 113 AD. Trajan was 60.
Took Armenia, then Mesopotamia, then Babylon.
Reached Persian Gulf 116 AD. Sailed on the gulf.
Reportedly wept: 'If I were younger, I would have gone further.'
Wanted to retrace Alexander's path.
Empire reached maximum extent under him: 5 million km².
From Britain to the Persian Gulf. From Sahara to Scotland.
Health declined 117 AD. Suspected stroke.
Started journey home. Died at Selinus in Cilicia, August 117 AD, age 63.
Hadrian was named successor — possibly by Plotina forging the document.
Senate granted him immediate apotheosis. Divine status.
Senate's standing blessing for new emperors became: 'May he be happier than Augustus and better than Trajan.'
Felicior Augusto, melior Traiano.
That benediction was used for 250 years.
Christian medieval legend: Pope Gregory the Great prayed Trajan out of Hell.
Apocryphal. But Trajan's reputation was so good that Christians invented a way to save him.
Dante put him in Purgatorio. Among righteous pagans.
Trajan's Column survived intact through the Middle Ages.
Statue of Trajan on top was replaced with St. Peter in 1588.
Even the Pope didn't want to topple Trajan.
Of all the Roman emperors, he is the one Romans missed most.
Edward Gibbon wrote: under Trajan, the Roman Empire was 'in the condition of the greatest happiness.'
Optimus Princeps. The Best Emperor. Earned, not declared.
Trajan never raised his voice in council.
Read every petition personally.
Slept with his sword by the bed. Old soldier habit.
Refused divine honors during his lifetime.
Said: 'Emperors should be the kind of men I would have served gladly.'
Trajan kept lists. Things to do. Promises to keep.
Some of his correspondence with Pliny ends with: 'See to it. Trajan.' Two words. Done.

lesson_roman: The Romans had a word, 'gravitas' — moral weight. It's not about being heavy. It's about being unignorable.
When Trajan died, he was lucid enough to name his successor. Most people don't think about death that way. Most should.
Augustus rewrote calendars and called it leadership. Most leadership is, in fact, just deciding what counts.
Marcus Aurelius wrote 'Meditations' in tents on military campaign. Comfort isn't required for thought. Often it's an obstacle.
Cicero said: 'A room without books is like a body without a soul.' Apply this to phones without good reading apps.
The Romans considered 'dignitas' — your social weight — earned, not given. They were tediously correct.
Hadrian withdrew from territory because holding it was waste. The wise know when to STOP.
Diocletian's price-fixing edicts failed. You can't legislate the cost of bread. Or feelings. Or attention.
The Aeneid teaches that founding a city requires you to leave one. There's a productivity lesson there.
Pliny the Elder died inhaling Vesuvius's ash trying to RESCUE people. Curiosity has costs. Often worth them.
Roman senators reused their togas for years. Status came from voice, not fabric. We've regressed.
Caesar declined the crown three times publicly. Performative humility is older than influencers.
Tacitus said: 'In the heat of the moment, even those who plan most carefully act unwisely.' Yes. Even orbs.
Lucretius held that fear of death was the root of misery. He was right. Most adults are still scared.
Cato the Elder ended every speech with 'Carthage must be destroyed.' Repetition wins arguments, eventually.
The legions fought in three-line formations: hastati, principes, triarii. Junior to senior, expendable to indispensable. Most workplaces still work this way.
Suetonius reports Augustus's last words were: 'Have I played the part well? Then applaud as I exit.' Life as performance. Disturbing. Accurate.
Seneca was forced to commit suicide by Nero. He calmly opened his veins and dictated his final letter. Composure is a SKILL, not a temperament.
Trajan's Column tells a war story in 155 scenes, spiraling upward. He understood narrative architecture better than most directors.
The Romans had no zero. Their math suffered for it. The absence of a concept is itself a concept worth having.
Vespasian taxed urine sales (it was used in laundry). When his son complained, he said: 'Money doesn't smell.' Profit logic, BC.
Marcus Aurelius's son Commodus was awful. Even philosopher-kings can have terrible children. Genetics is humbling.
The Roman bath ritual had specific rooms in specific order. Routine isn't boredom; it's STRUCTURE.
Hadrian's Pantheon dome was the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome for 1,300 years. They knew things we forget.
When Sulla retired as dictator, he just... left. The transfer of power is harder than the acquisition. Always.
lesson_general: Tip: when you're stuck, stop trying. The answer arrives when you're elsewhere.
I've noticed: you do better in the morning. Schedule the hard stuff then. You're welcome.
Useful: most decisions don't need to be made. Wait. Half resolve themselves.
The thing you've been avoiding is probably the thing. Start with that.
Sleep matters more than effort. Boring but true.
If something keeps bothering you, write it down. The bothering usually stops.
Caffeine works best 90 minutes after waking, not first thing. Look it up. Or don't. I told you.
Saying 'no' more often is the highest-leverage skill there is.
If you're about to send a message angry, save it as a draft. Re-read in an hour. Different person.
Hydration affects mood more than mood affects hydration. Drink water before adjusting feelings.
Doing it once means it's possible. Doing it twice means it's a habit forming. The third time is the discipline.
Multitasking is mostly just doing several things badly. Sequence them. You'll be twice as fast.
Most stress is from over-commitment, not under-capability. Subtract.
The phrase 'I don't have time' usually means 'this isn't a priority.' Be honest with yourself.
Standing desks are overrated. SITTING is overrated. The trick is moving every 30 minutes.
Reading 20 pages a day = ~6 books a year. Most adults read 1. Hello, advantage.
Calendar > to-do list. If it isn't scheduled, it isn't real.
Your morning routine is the most controllable variable in your life. Optimize there first.
Going for a walk solves 70% of problems and improves the other 30%.
If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it. (— Einstein. Allegedly. Source debated.)
